ctaText: See how we build AI solutions

questions.question: What does 'RAG' stand for in LLM applications?
options: Rapid Application Generation
Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Real-time Adaptive Gateway
Recursive Agent Graph
questions.explanation: RAG combines retrieval systems with LLMs to ground responses in external knowledge, reducing hallucinations and improving factual accuracy.

questions.question: In the Transformer architecture, what is the primary purpose of the self-attention mechanism?
options: To compress the input sequence into a fixed-size vector
To allow each token to attend to every other token in the sequence
To generate random noise for regularization
To convert words into one-hot encoded vectors
questions.explanation: Self-attention lets each token compute a weighted sum over all positions in the input, capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence.

questions.question: What does the 'temperature' parameter control during LLM text generation?
options: The maximum number of tokens in the output
The randomness of the probability distribution over next tokens
The learning rate during fine-tuning
The size of the context window
questions.explanation: Temperature scales the logits before softmax: lower values make the distribution sharper (more deterministic), higher values make it flatter (more creative/random).

questions.question: What is a 'token' in the context of large language models?
options: A complete sentence processed as one unit
A sub-word unit produced by algorithms like BPE or SentencePiece
A binary encoding of each character
A unique identifier for each training document
questions.explanation: Tokenizers like Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) split text into sub-word units, balancing vocabulary size and the ability to represent any text.

questions.question: What is 'prompt engineering' primarily concerned with?
options: Modifying the model's internal weights
Crafting effective input instructions to elicit desired model behaviour
Building the GPU infrastructure for model training
Designing the tokenizer vocabulary
questions.explanation: Prompt engineering focuses on structuring inputs, providing context, and using techniques like few-shot examples to guide model outputs without changing weights.

questions.question: What is a common cause of 'hallucination' in LLMs?
options: Insufficient GPU memory during inference
The model generating plausible-sounding text not grounded in its training data or provided context
Using too low a temperature setting
Exceeding the token rate limit
questions.explanation: Hallucinations occur when models produce fluent but factually incorrect content because they rely on statistical patterns rather than verified knowledge.

questions.question: What is the 'context window' of an LLM?
options: The time it takes to process a single request
The maximum number of tokens the model can consider in a single forward pass
The number of layers in the transformer network
The amount of training data used
questions.explanation: The context window defines the upper limit of combined input and output tokens. Exceeding it causes the model to lose earlier information.

questions.question: What are embeddings in the context of AI/NLP?
options: Physical hardware chips embedded in servers
Dense numerical vector representations that capture semantic meaning
Encryption keys used to secure model weights
Log files generated during model training
questions.explanation: Embeddings map text (words, sentences, or documents) into continuous vector spaces where semantic similarity corresponds to geometric proximity.

questions.question: What is the role of an AI 'agent' in modern LLM systems?
options: A human reviewer who checks model outputs
An autonomous system that uses an LLM to plan, reason, and call external tools to accomplish goals
A smaller model that compresses large model weights
A database that stores all training data
questions.explanation: AI agents combine LLM reasoning with tool-use capabilities, enabling autonomous multi-step task completion through planning and execution loops.

questions.question: What does RLHF stand for, and why is it used?
options: Randomized Linear Hyperparameter Fitting; it tunes batch sizes automatically
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback; it aligns model outputs with human preferences
Recursive Layer Hashing Framework; it speeds up inference
Real-time Language Handling Format; it standardizes API responses
questions.explanation: RLHF trains a reward model from human preference data and uses reinforcement learning to fine-tune the LLM so its outputs better align with human expectations.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a vector database in AI applications?
options: To store relational data with SQL queries
To efficiently index and retrieve high-dimensional embedding vectors using similarity search
To compress model checkpoints for deployment
To manage user authentication tokens
questions.explanation: Vector databases like Pinecone, Weaviate, and Qdrant enable fast approximate nearest-neighbour search over embeddings, powering RAG and semantic search systems.

questions.question: What is model quantization?
options: Splitting a model across multiple GPUs for parallel training
Reducing the numerical precision of model weights (e.g., from FP32 to INT4) to decrease size and speed up inference
Adding more layers to increase model capacity
Converting a text model into a multimodal model
questions.explanation: Quantization trades small accuracy losses for significant reductions in memory footprint and inference latency, enabling large models to run on smaller hardware.

questions.question: What is 'chain-of-thought' prompting?
options: Connecting multiple LLMs in a sequential pipeline
Asking the model to show its step-by-step reasoning before giving the final answer
Training the model on chains of related documents
A method for encrypting prompts in transit
questions.explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting improves accuracy on reasoning tasks by encouraging the model to decompose problems into intermediate steps before arriving at a conclusion.

questions.question: What is model distillation?
options: Removing toxic content from training data
Training a smaller 'student' model to replicate the behaviour of a larger 'teacher' model
Converting a generative model into a discriminative one
Splitting a model into microservices for deployment
questions.explanation: Knowledge distillation transfers the learned behaviour of a large, expensive model into a compact model that is cheaper and faster to run with minimal quality loss.

questions.question: What is the key difference between fine-tuning and prompt engineering?
options: Fine-tuning is free; prompt engineering requires expensive hardware
Fine-tuning updates model weights on new data; prompt engineering modifies only the input without changing weights
Prompt engineering requires more data than fine-tuning
There is no practical difference; they achieve the same result
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning adjusts internal weights through additional training on task-specific data, while prompt engineering achieves adaptation purely through clever input design.
